Description: Optimal average power for ceramic cleaning

Pulse frequency for controlled material removal
Energy per unit area for surface cleaning
Pulse duration for minimal thermal penetration
Beam travel speed for uniform coverage
Number of cleaning cycles for complete removal

Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of hard coatings like TiN to ablate contaminants without substrate melting (TiN melting point ~2950°C). Starting at 100W for controlled energy delivery; max limited to avoid thermal damage. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs for 1064 nm cleaning systems; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Metallic Substrates' (Optics and Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

Effective speeds balance ablation efficiency and heat accumulation on TiN's moderate thermal conductivity (~20 W/m·K). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal contaminant removal; slower (100-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (2000-5000 mm/s) for light. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Laser Applications (2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning of TiN to achieve photothermal ablation with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm). Picosecond (ps, 1-100 ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduce HAZ further but increase cost; 50 ns recommended for balance. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019) on TiN ablation thresholds at 1064 nm.

Typical for pulsed cleaning to ensure pulse overlap without excessive heat buildup on reflective TiN surfaces (reflectivity ~40% at 1064 nm). 50 kHz recommended for TiN to speed up process while monitoring temperature; higher rates (>100 kHz) risk accumulation, lower for precision. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017) on pulse repetition effects in coating cleaning.

Fluence range for safe ablation of contaminants on TiN; damage threshold ~2-3 J/cm² due to high melting point and conductivity. Recommended 1.0 J/cm² for effective cleaning without delamination. Conservative estimate for industrial use. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2021) for TiN at 1064 nm.

Beam diameter suited for TiN surface preparation; smaller (50-100 μm) for fine details, larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage on coated surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy or layered removal on TiN to avoid over-ablation. Recommended 2 passes for complete, damage-free cleaning. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Surface & Coatings Technology (2019).

Ensures uniform fluence distribution on TiN for even cleaning; 60% recommended to prevent gaps or hotspots given beam Gaussian profile. Typical range 30-80% based on scan speed. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2022).
